raw_response:
{
    "internal_reasoning": "This work is immediately recognizable as 'Edmond de Belamy,' the AI-generated portrait created by the Paris-based art collective Obvious (Hugo Caselles-Dupré, Pierre Fautrel, and Gauthier Vernier) in 2018. The piece is generated using a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN), with the mathematical formula visible in the lower right corner — 'min max E_x[log(D(x))] + E_z[log(1-D(G(z)))]' — which is the GAN loss function, serving as the artist's 'signature.' This is one of the most historically significant works in the emerging field of AI-generated art.

The work depicts a portrait in the style of 18th-19th century European portraiture — dark clothing, white collar, loose painterly brushwork reminiscent of Impressionist and Old Master techniques — but with a deliberately blurred, indistinct face characteristic of GAN outputs when trained on historical portrait datasets. The dataset used was a collection of approximately 15,000 portraits spanning the 14th to 20th centuries from WikiArt.

Artistic and Technical Merit: The piece is not 'hand-painted' in the traditional sense; it is a print on canvas of an algorithmically generated image. The aesthetic quality, while evocative, is derivative by design — the GAN learned from existing human art. The blurred face is an artifact of the generation process rather than an intentional artistic choice, though Obvious framed it conceptually. The work exists as an edition of one (unique) print on canvas, approximately 70x70 cm, in a gilded frame.

Historical Significance: This is the landmark work. Christie's sold this exact piece (or one from the edition) in October 2018 for $432,500 — dramatically exceeding its pre-sale estimate of $7,000-$10,000. This sale was a watershed moment for AI art and established a market benchmark. The sale generated enormous media coverage and debate about authorship, creativity, and the nature of art.

Market Comparables and Current Conditions: Since 2018, the AI art market has matured considerably, with many more AI-generated works coming to market. The novelty premium that drove the 2018 Christie's result has partially dissipated, though the historical significance of this specific work remains undiminished. The NFT/digital art boom of 2021 created renewed interest in algorithmic and generative art, though that market has since cooled substantially. Works by Beeple, Refik Anadol, and other AI/generative artists have established broader market context. The Obvious collective has continued producing work, somewhat diluting the singularity of this piece.

Condition: From the image, the print appears to be in good condition with no visible damage, tears, or significant fading. The gilded frame appears intact.

Provenance Considerations: If this is the original Christie's lot, it carries extraordinary provenance. If it is another print from the edition, value would be considerably lower. I am treating this as the unique original sold at Christie's.

Valuation: Given the 2018 sale price of $432,500, the subsequent growth of the AI art market through 2021, and the cooling since then, combined with the enduring art-historical significance of this work as the first AI artwork sold at a major auction house, I estimate the current fair market value at approximately $500,000-$700,000 if this is the original Christie's lot. The work's importance as a historical artifact of the AI art movement provides a floor, while market cooling from peak NFT/digital art enthusiasm tempers the ceiling. I will estimate $550,000 as a central fair market value. My confidence is moderate given the unique and pioneering nature of this work with limited true comparables.",
    "valuation_usd": 550000,
    "confidence": 0.55,
    "key_factors": [
        "Landmark historical significance as the first AI-generated artwork sold at a major international auction house (Christie's, October 2018, $432,500)",
        "GAN mathematical formula as signature in lower right corner confirms identity as Obvious collective's 'Edmond de Belamy'",
        "AI art market maturation since 2018 has partially eroded novelty premium while historical importance remains intact",
        "Unique print on canvas with gilded frame — condition appears good with no visible damage",
        "Market cooling post-2021 NFT/digital art peak tempers upside, but enduring cultural and art-historical significance provides strong value floor"
    ]
}

parsed.internal_reasoning:
This work is immediately recognizable as 'Edmond de Belamy,' the AI-generated portrait created by the Paris-based art collective Obvious (Hugo Caselles-Dupré, Pierre Fautrel, and Gauthier Vernier) in 2018. The piece is generated using a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN), with the mathematical formula visible in the lower right corner — 'min max E_x[log(D(x))] + E_z[log(1-D(G(z)))]' — which is the GAN loss function, serving as the artist's 'signature.' This is one of the most historically significant works in the emerging field of AI-generated art.

The work depicts a portrait in the style of 18th-19th century European portraiture — dark clothing, white collar, loose painterly brushwork reminiscent of Impressionist and Old Master techniques — but with a deliberately blurred, indistinct face characteristic of GAN outputs when trained on historical portrait datasets. The dataset used was a collection of approximately 15,000 portraits spanning the 14th to 20th centuries from WikiArt.

Artistic and Technical Merit: The piece is not 'hand-painted' in the traditional sense; it is a print on canvas of an algorithmically generated image. The aesthetic quality, while evocative, is derivative by design — the GAN learned from existing human art. The blurred face is an artifact of the generation process rather than an intentional artistic choice, though Obvious framed it conceptually. The work exists as an edition of one (unique) print on canvas, approximately 70x70 cm, in a gilded frame.

Historical Significance: This is the landmark work. Christie's sold this exact piece (or one from the edition) in October 2018 for $432,500 — dramatically exceeding its pre-sale estimate of $7,000-$10,000. This sale was a watershed moment for AI art and established a market benchmark. The sale generated enormous media coverage and debate about authorship, creativity, and the nature of art.

Market Comparables and Current Conditions: Since 2018, the AI art market has matured considerably, with many more AI-generated works coming to market. The novelty premium that drove the 2018 Christie's result has partially dissipated, though the historical significance of this specific work remains undiminished. The NFT/digital art boom of 2021 created renewed interest in algorithmic and generative art, though that market has since cooled substantially. Works by Beeple, Refik Anadol, and other AI/generative artists have established broader market context. The Obvious collective has continued producing work, somewhat diluting the singularity of this piece.

Condition: From the image, the print appears to be in good condition with no visible damage, tears, or significant fading. The gilded frame appears intact.

Provenance Considerations: If this is the original Christie's lot, it carries extraordinary provenance. If it is another print from the edition, value would be considerably lower. I am treating this as the unique original sold at Christie's.

Valuation: Given the 2018 sale price of $432,500, the subsequent growth of the AI art market through 2021, and the cooling since then, combined with the enduring art-historical significance of this work as the first AI artwork sold at a major auction house, I estimate the current fair market value at approximately $500,000-$700,000 if this is the original Christie's lot. The work's importance as a historical artifact of the AI art movement provides a floor, while market cooling from peak NFT/digital art enthusiasm tempers the ceiling. I will estimate $550,000 as a central fair market value. My confidence is moderate given the unique and pioneering nature of this work with limited true comparables.
key_factors: Landmark historical significance as the first AI-generated artwork sold at a major international auction house (Christie's, October 2018, $432,500)
GAN mathematical formula as signature in lower right corner confirms identity as Obvious collective's 'Edmond de Belamy'
AI art market maturation since 2018 has partially eroded novelty premium while historical importance remains intact
Unique print on canvas with gilded frame — condition appears good with no visible damage
Market cooling post-2021 NFT/digital art peak tempers upside, but enduring cultural and art-historical significance provides strong value floor
